title: The Guardian - July 24, 1990 (Page 34)
author: Unknown
card_summary: A newspaper clipping from The Guardian (London, England), dated July 24, 1990, page 34. Likely a feature, review, or retrospective related to Philip K. Dick or adaptations of his work, published eight years after his death during a period of growing international recognition.
page_summary:
A scanned newspaper page from The Guardian (London, England), dated Tuesday, July 24, 1990, page 34. The clipping was sourced from Newspapers.com and downloaded on April 9, 2025. The PDF text layer contains only source metadata with no recoverable article text.

The Guardian is one of Britain's leading quality newspapers, and its coverage of Dick reflects the strong tradition of taking his work seriously in the United Kingdom. By July 1990, Total Recall had just been released in cinemas, generating renewed interest in Dick's work. British literary culture, through publishers like Gollancz and critics in outlets like The Guardian and New Statesman, played a crucial role in establishing Dick's reputation as a serious literary figure, often ahead of American critical recognition.
